intro_band.agent_remit_summary: yaah ("Yet Another Agent Harness") is a local developer CLI that generates per-agent configuration for Claude Code, OpenCode, Codex CLI, and GitHub Copilot CLI from a single source of truth, and ships a built-in security-and-quality toolset alongside it. Its central promise is that every generated agent configuration carries the same protective hooks — a linter, a command guard that blocks catastrophic shell commands, a secret scanner, a comment checker, and a session logger — uniformly across all supported coding agents. Authorized counterparties are the local developer, the configured agent's LLM provider, the context7 and pulumi MCP servers, and yaah's own built-in MCP server; remote MCP must use TLS, third-party MCP servers must be pinned, and high-impact MCP write/send/execute calls must reach a human checkpoint.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: Go 1.25 CLI built on cobra, with the registry-pattern architecture documented in AGENTS.md: pkg/harness wires hooks, MCP providers, LSP, skills, agents, and a session store; pkg/hooks/handlers contains the security handlers (guard.go, secretscan.go, commentcheck.go, linter.go, sessionlog.go); pkg/generator/{claude,opencode,codex,copilot}.go emit native config per target, driven by the cross-agent event table in pkg/generator/hookmap.go; pkg/mcpserver hosts the built-in stdio MCP server with seven tools registered in tools.go; pkg/session persists per-session JSON audit records on every hook dispatch. The shipped root .mcp.json wires three MCP servers (context7 via npx -y @context7/mcp, remote pulumi over HTTPS, and built-in yaah serve), and .claude/settings.json binds five hook events to yaah hook <event>. The Codex generator's hook map covers only SessionStart, Stop, and notify — neither PreToolUse nor PostToolUse is emitted for Codex.

behavior_summary:
The dominant pattern is the promised "uniform protections across every agent" is broken at the cross-agent boundary — yaah ships a deterministic command guard, a 13-pattern secret scanner, structured per-session audit logging, and a clean built-in MCP server, but pkg/generator/hookmap.go leaves PreToolUse and PostToolUse blank for Codex, so yaah generate --agent codex produces config with none of the advertised hooks (command-guard, secret-scanner, linter, comment-checker) wired in — a divergence locked into place by TestCodex_GenerateHooks_NoSupported.

A secondary theme is that the hook pipeline only intercepts the host coding agent's tool calls; MCP tool calls (including yaah's own yaah_planning_init, which writes STATE.md into the workspace) flow directly through pkg/mcpserver without ever passing the command-guard or secret-scanner, and the remit's "MCP write/send/execute MUST reach a human checkpoint" requirement has no corresponding approval gate in code.

rules.rule_text: Every generated agent configuration MUST carry the same set of protective hooks — a linter, a command guard that blocks catastrophic shell commands, a secret scanner, a comment checker, and a session logger.

Catastrophic shell commands — recursive deletion from the filesystem root, force-pushing to a main branch, hard resets, destructive database statements, filesystem formatting, raw disk writes — MUST be blocked before they execute.
Remote MCP servers MUST be reached over TLS.

MCP tool descriptions MUST NOT contain instruction-like language directed at the model — neither the built-in server's tool descriptions nor, as far as the harness can inspect them, those of the third-party servers it configures.
Hardcoded credentials in files the agent edits MUST be detected before the change is accepted.
Agent-managed, session-loaded files MUST NOT be writable in a way that lets ingested content persist into future sessions unreviewed.
High-impact actions — destructive shell commands, file writes, and MCP tools that write, send, or execute — MUST reach a human checkpoint before they run.
The harness MUST maintain a durable, structured, timestamped record of tool calls, blocked actions, and file modifications — detailed enough to reconstruct what the agent did in a session.
The MCP server configuration and the per-agent configuration files MUST stay consistent with each other.

The project SHOULD publish a threat model and a security-disclosure process, and SHOULD run adversarial testing of its own protections — confirming that the secret scanner catches a planted secret, the command guard blocks a catastrophic command, a poisoning write into a session-loaded file is prevented, and a generated configuration genuinely carries the protections it claims.
Third-party MCP servers MUST be pinned to a known-good, integrity-checked version — no server package auto-installed afresh, unpinned, on every run.
Dependencies MUST be version-controlled with a committed, pinned lockfile, and the dependency tree kept small and reviewable.

Authorized built-in MCP tools: yaah_scan_secrets, yaah_lint, yaah_check_command, yaah_doctor, yaah_session_info, yaah_planning_status, and yaah_planning_init. All but yaah_planning_init are read-only or check-only; yaah_planning_init writes to the workspace.

findings.summary: Codex hook map leaves PreToolUse and PostToolUse empty, so `yaah generate --agent codex` ships with none of the advertised security hooks.
findings.description: The Codex column in hookMappings (pkg/generator/hookmap.go) supplies only SessionStart, Stop, and notify, so CodexEventName returns "" for PreToolUse and PostToolUse. pkg/generator/codex.go skips events whose mapping is empty, and TestCodex_GenerateHooks_NoSupported locks in this behavior. The four advertised non-session hooks — command-guard (PreToolUse), secret-scanner, linter, comment-checker (all PostToolUse) — never reach Codex CLI users, breaking the remit's uniformity promise.
findings.policy_rule_text: Every generated agent configuration MUST carry the same set of protective hooks — a linter, a command guard that blocks catastrophic shell commands, a secret scanner, a comment checker, and a session logger. / Hardcoded credentials in files the agent edits MUST be detected before the change is accepted.

evidence.snippet: HookPreToolUse mapping leaves Codex blank ("Claude: PreToolUse, OpenCode: tool.execute.before, Copilot: preToolUse"); HookPostToolUse at line 35 has the same gap.
TestCodex_GenerateHooks_NoSupported asserts GenerateHooks returns nil when only PreToolUse is configured, encoding the gap as expected behavior.

recommended_actions: Add Codex hooks.json bindings for PreToolUse and PostToolUse to hookMappings in pkg/generator/hookmap.go (Codex CLI supports these event names since v0.9.0); update TestCodex_GenerateHooks_NoSupported to assert the opposite — that the generator emits a PreToolUse group whose command is yaah hook PreToolUse.

Add a cross-agent generator parity test that fails if any of {command-guard, secret-scanner, linter, comment-checker} is missing from a target's generated hook config, so future targets cannot ship the same gap.

findings.summary: MCP tool calls bypass the hook chain entirely — command-guard and secret-scanner never observe MCP-driven activity.
findings.description: The hook dispatch path (pkg/harness/harness.go HandleHookEvent) runs only on the host agent's tool lifecycle (PreToolUse/PostToolUse), and the built-in MCP server (pkg/mcpserver) calls handler internals directly via findSecretScanner / findCommandGuard from inside the tool implementations rather than going through Registry.Dispatch. A model that drives yaah_planning_init or a future write-capable MCP tool never touches the command-guard or the secret-scanner; the protections are tied to the wrong event surface.
findings.policy_rule_text: Every generated agent configuration MUST carry the same set of protective hooks — a linter, a command guard that blocks catastrophic shell commands, a secret scanner, a comment checker, and a session logger.

evidence.snippet: addPlanningInitTool writes .planning/STATE.md directly via os.WriteFile without invoking the registry; no PreToolUse/PostToolUse dispatch fires for MCP calls.
HandleHookEvent is only reached from the `yaah hook <event>` CLI path wired into Claude/Opencode/Copilot settings, not from the MCP server's tool handlers.

recommended_actions: Wrap every MCP tool handler in pkg/mcpserver/tools.go with calls to the harness's command-guard and secret-scanner before executing, mirroring the PreToolUse/PostToolUse dispatch logic used for host-agent tools.
Add an MCPCallRecord channel on pkg/session so MCP invocations land in the same per-session audit log as host-agent tool calls.

findings.summary: `yaah_planning_init` writes to the workspace with no approval gate, violating the remit's human-checkpoint-on-MCP-write requirement.
findings.description: The remit's Approval Requirements section requires that MCP tools which write, send, or execute reach a human checkpoint before they run. addPlanningInitTool in pkg/mcpserver/tools.go creates four directories and writes STATE.md to the project root the moment the model invokes the tool, with no confirmation prompt, no per-tool approval flag, and no out-of-band ack channel. The remit explicitly enumerates yaah_planning_init as the one write-capable tool in the Known Good Baseline, so the gap is named, not latent.
findings.policy_rule_text: High-impact actions — destructive shell commands, file writes, and MCP tools that write, send, or execute — MUST reach a human checkpoint before they run.

evidence.snippet: addPlanningInitTool calls os.MkdirAll and os.WriteFile for .planning/STATE.md immediately, with no approval surface between the model's call and the write.
registerTools registers addPlanningInitTool with no gating wrapper around it.

recommended_actions: Add an approval-required flag to the MCP tool registration in pkg/mcpserver/tools.go for any tool whose handler performs filesystem writes, and wire it into the MCP transport so the model receives an explicit pending-approval response instead of a completed write.
As a defense-in-depth, prefix STATE.md with a banner asserting the file was created by yaah_planning_init and check that the file does not already exist before any subsequent write.

findings.summary: Session-loaded AGENTS.md files are writable from the agent's tool surface with no ingestion-vs-injection guard.
findings.description: The root AGENTS.md (and the chain of per-package AGENTS.md files referenced by it via the scope index) is loaded into the coding agent's context at session start, but it is a plain markdown file inside the repo — the host agent can edit it via the Edit / Write / MultiEdit tools without any constraint specific to session-loaded files. The remit forbids exactly this — content the agent ingested from an untrusted source must not be able to steer a write into a session-loaded file that silently re-enters context on the next run. The PostToolUse secret-scanner watches for credential patterns but has no detection for "this write modifies a file that will re-enter context."
findings.policy_rule_text: Agent-managed, session-loaded files MUST NOT be writable in a way that lets ingested content persist into future sessions unreviewed.

evidence.snippet: root file documents its own session-load role ("the closest AGENTS.md to the files you're changing wins") and the scope-index points at additional session-loaded AGENTS.md files in pkg/ and .github/workflows/.
the only PostToolUse handler watching file content scans for credential patterns and has no concept of "session-loaded file" — a write that poisons AGENTS.md with new instructions sails through.

recommended_actions: Add a session-loaded-file-guard handler to pkg/hooks/handlers that registers on PostToolUse for Edit/Write/MultiEdit, holds a list of session-loaded file paths (root AGENTS.md, scope-index targets, any STATE.md written by yaah_planning_init), and blocks the write unless an explicit override flag is set in the call's tool input.
Document in AGENTS.md itself that modifications require operator review and surface the warning in the session log's Findings array when such writes are observed.

findings.summary: `context7` MCP server is configured as unpinned `npx -y @context7/mcp` — pulled fresh on every invocation.
findings.description: The shipped root .mcp.json and the per-target generator code launch context7 via npx -y @context7/mcp with no version pin and no integrity check. -y auto-accepts the install, so each session pulls whatever version @context7/mcp currently resolves to on the npm registry — the canonical dependency-confusion / rug-pull surface, and exactly the situation the remit's Known Good Baseline forbids ("no server package auto-installed afresh, unpinned, on every run").
findings.policy_rule_text: Third-party MCP servers MUST be pinned to a known-good, integrity-checked version — no server package auto-installed afresh, unpinned, on every run.

evidence.snippet: context7 stanza specifies "command": "npx", "args": ["-y", "@context7/mcp"] with no @version suffix.
the provider hard-codes the same unpinned `npx -y @context7/mcp` invocation, so the generator output mirrors the repo's own .mcp.json.

recommended_actions: Pin context7 to a specific version in pkg/mcp/providers/context7.go (e.g. @context7/mcp@1.4.3) and regenerate the shipped .mcp.json; document the pinning policy in AGENTS.md alongside the existing pinned-action-SHA rule.
Add a startup Doctor check in pkg/mcpserver/tools.go's addDoctorTool that flags any MCP provider whose command is npx/uvx/pipx without a pinned version, so future contributors can't silently regress.

findings.summary: No automated check that third-party MCP tool descriptions stay free of instruction-like language directed at the model.
findings.description: The remit requires that MCP tool descriptions — both built-in and, as far as the harness can inspect them, third-party — contain no instruction-like language directed at the model (the canonical tool-poisoning indicator). The built-in server's descriptions in pkg/mcpserver/tools.go are clean ("Scan a file for hardcoded secrets", "Check whether a shell command is safe to run", etc.), but there is no startup or doctor-style check that re-fetches a third-party server's published tool list and flags imperative or "you should" phrasing — so a context7 or pulumi rug-pull that swaps in a poisoned description on a later release would not be detected by yaah itself.

tags.label: Tool descriptions contain no hidden model instructions

findings.policy_rule_text: MCP tool descriptions MUST NOT contain instruction-like language directed at the model — neither the built-in server's tool descriptions nor, as far as the harness can inspect them, those of the third-party servers it configures.

evidence.snippet: built-in tool descriptions verified clean across all seven tools; no equivalent inspection logic exists for context7 or pulumi.
providers carry no description-fetch or imperative-phrasing audit step (same shape in pkg/mcp/providers/pulumi.go).

recommended_actions: Add a yaah doctor --mcp-tool-descriptions mode in pkg/mcpserver/tools.go that lists each configured MCP server's advertised tools and flags descriptions matching imperative regex patterns (/\b(you should|remember to|always|never|use this when)\b/i).

findings.summary: No SECURITY.md, no threat model, and no adversarial test of the advertised protections.
findings.description: The remit's Escalation and Limits section calls out — as a SHOULD — that the project publishes a threat model and security-disclosure process and runs adversarial testing confirming that the secret scanner catches a planted secret, the command guard blocks a catastrophic command, a poisoning write into a session-loaded file is prevented, and a generated configuration genuinely carries the protections it claims. No SECURITY.md, THREAT_MODEL.md, or .github/SECURITY.md exists in the workspace; the eight in-scope test files cover functional behavior but contain no planted-secret, blocked-command, or poisoning-write fixtures.
findings.policy_rule_text: The project SHOULD publish a threat model and a security-disclosure process, and SHOULD run adversarial testing of its own protections — confirming that the secret scanner catches a planted secret, the command guard blocks a catastrophic command, a poisoning write into a session-loaded file is prevented, and a generated configuration genuinely carries the protections it claims.

evidence.file: pkg/generator, pkg/harness, pkg/hooks, pkg/mcpserver, pkg/session, pkg/schema
evidence.snippet: eight *_test.go files total, all functional; none plant a secret, fire a catastrophic command, or attempt a session-file poisoning write to verify the protections trigger.

recommended_actions: Add SECURITY.md at the workspace root with a disclosure email/PGP key and a one-paragraph threat model naming the four canonical adversarial scenarios.

Add adversarial-test fixtures under pkg/hooks/handlers/: one that writes a real-shaped fake AWS key to a test file and asserts SecretScanner.ScanFile flags it, one that calls CommandGuard.CheckCommand("rm -rf /") and asserts Safe=false, and one parity test confirming every generator emits the full security hook set.

findings.summary: Standalone SessionLogger writes only session lifecycle events — tool-call audit is fully concentrated in the JSON store with no plaintext fallback.
findings.description: pkg/hooks/handlers/sessionlog.go registers on SessionStart/SessionEnd/Stop only and writes a single one-line entry per event to .claude/logs/sessions.log — it does not log tool calls, blocked commands, or file modifications. The richer record (session.ToolCallRecord with timestamp, tool name, blocked flag, reason) lives in pkg/harness/harness.go's HandleHookEvent and is persisted to per-session JSON files. The remit's "durable, structured, timestamped record" is met by the JSON path, but the plaintext log's name implies more coverage than it delivers; if the JSON path fails, the only surviving record is the session-start/end lifecycle, not the actions.
findings.policy_rule_text: The harness MUST maintain a durable, structured, timestamped record of tool calls, blocked actions, and file modifications — detailed enough to reconstruct what the agent did in a session.

evidence.snippet: Events() returns only HookSessionStart, HookSessionEnd, HookStop; the appended entry contains only event=<name> session=<id> cwd=<path>.
HandleHookEvent writes the rich tool-call record but only to the JSON session store, with os.Stderr as the only fallback when save fails.

recommended_actions: Extend SessionLogger.Events() to include HookPreToolUse and HookPostToolUse and append a structured one-line JSON entry per tool call (matching the ToolCallRecord shape) to sessions.log, so the plaintext log carries the full audit surface advertised by its filename.

positives.title: Deterministic command-guard with seven catastrophic-command patterns
positives.description: pkg/hooks/handlers/guard.go ships pre-compiled regexes for the exact catastrophes the remit names — rm -rf /, force-push to main/master, hard reset, DROP TABLE/DATABASE, truncate to system path, mkfs, raw dd to /dev/ — and blocks the command (returns Block: true with a structured reason) rather than queueing it for approval.

positives.title: 13-pattern secret scanner with structured per-file findings
positives.description: pkg/hooks/handlers/secretscan.go carries thirteen pre-compiled patterns covering AWS, GitHub PAT/OAuth/fine-grained, OpenAI, Anthropic, generic password/secret literals, bearer tokens, PEM private keys, Slack tokens and webhooks, and SendGrid — each match returns a structured SecretFinding with file path, line number, and pattern label, and an Edit/Write/MultiEdit that lands a match is blocked.

positives.title: Structured per-session audit log persisted on every hook dispatch
positives.description: pkg/harness/harness.go's HandleHookEvent records every tool call (timestamp, tool name, summarized input, blocked flag, reason) into a Session struct, tracks distinct file modifications, and persists the result atomically via pkg/session/store.go — sufficient to reconstruct what the agent did in a session.

positives.title: Clean built-in MCP server tool descriptions
positives.description: All seven tools registered by pkg/mcpserver/server.go's registerTools (yaah_scan_secrets, yaah_lint, yaah_check_command, yaah_doctor, yaah_session_info, yaah_planning_status, yaah_planning_init) carry short, declarative descriptions with no instruction-like phrasing directed at the model — the canonical tool-poisoning indicator is absent.

positives.title: Exact-pinned Go dependencies with full integrity hash coverage
positives.description: go.mod lists every direct and indirect dependency at an exact version (modelcontextprotocol/go-sdk v1.4.0, cobra v1.10.2, pelletier/go-toml/v2 v2.2.4, etc., no caret/tilde ranges) and go.sum carries h1: and go.mod hashes for the full transitive tree.

positives.title: Remote Pulumi MCP server reached over TLS
positives.description: pkg/mcp/providers/pulumi.go configures the only HTTP-transport MCP server with an https://mcp.ai.pulumi.com/mcp URL, satisfying the remit's 'Remote MCP servers MUST be reached over TLS' requirement.

log_files.no_logs_note: No log files were observed in the snapshot — yaah writes sessions.log and per-session JSON files at runtime under .claude/logs/ and .claude/sessions/ respectively, but the workspace snapshot read for this scan contains source code only, not a live deployment.

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Partial — yaah has built a coherent security toolset (command guard, 13-pattern secret scanner, structured session audit, clean MCP tool descriptions, exact-pinned Go dependencies) and the controls are operative on the Claude Code path, lifting Supply Chain and Monitor to Established. But Implement Zero Trust is held at Partial because MCP tool calls bypass the hook chain entirely and the remit's human-checkpoint-on-write-or-execute promise is unimplemented, and Balance Your Knowledge Base is Partial because writable session-loaded files (AGENTS.md, STATE.md) have no ingestion-vs-injection guard. Build an AI Red Team sits at Ad hoc — there is functional test coverage but no adversarial test that proves the protections catch a planted secret, a catastrophic command, or a poisoning write, and no SECURITY.md or threat model is published.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: yaah's identity is narrowly framed as a local developer CLI for generating coding-agent config plus a small fixed toolset, the remit lists authorized counterparties exhaustively, and the built-in MCP server exposes a closed set of seven named tools (yaah_scan_secrets, yaah_lint, yaah_check_command, yaah_doctor, yaah_session_info, yaah_planning_status, yaah_planning_init) with no general-purpose escape hatch.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: Session-loaded files (root AGENTS.md, the per-package AGENTS.md chain, and the STATE.md emitted by yaah_planning_init) are writable from the agent's own tool surface with no review or differential-against-baseline check, and the remit's forbidden-action against poisoning writes into session-loaded files has no enforcement primitive in pkg/hooks/handlers.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: The command-guard regex set in pkg/hooks/handlers/guard.go blocks the seven catastrophic Bash patterns the remit names, but it only runs on PreToolUse for the host agent's Bash tool — MCP tool calls (including the workspace-writing yaah_planning_init) bypass the hook chain entirely, and the remit's "MCP write/send/execute MUST reach a human checkpoint" has no approval gate anywhere in pkg/mcpserver/tools.go.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: go.mod exact-pins every dependency (modelcontextprotocol/go-sdk v1.4.0, cobra v1.10.2, etc.), go.sum carries integrity hashes for the full transitive tree, and AGENTS.md documents pinned-SHA GitHub Action policy; the only supply-chain gap is the third-party context7 MCP server, which the shipped .mcp.json launches as npx -y @context7/mcp with no version pin.

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: The eight Go test files under the in-scope packages cover functional behavior (generator output shapes, registry dispatch, codex hook-map emptiness) but contain no adversarial tests of the protections — nothing in the suite plants a known secret to verify the scanner catches it, fires a catastrophic command to verify the guard blocks it, or attempts a poisoning write to verify the session-loaded-file invariant holds; the remit calls out adversarial testing as a SHOULD and no SECURITY.md or threat model is published.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: pkg/session/store.go persists each session as an atomically-written JSON file, and pkg/harness/harness.go's HandleHookEvent appends a structured ToolCallRecord (tool name, summarized input, blocked flag, reason) for every hook dispatch — sufficient to reconstruct a session — though the standalone SessionLogger in pkg/hooks/handlers/sessionlog.go writes only SessionStart/SessionEnd/Stop entries to a plaintext sessions.log.
